title: L07 · Modal Verbs
objective: Master the six modal verbs (können, wollen, müssen, sollen, dürfen, mögen) and the unique word-order rule they trigger: the SECOND verb goes to the END of the sentence in infinitive form.
strategy: Modal verbs are slightly irregular but follow a clean pattern: ich and er/sie/es have the SAME form. Learn that quirk first, then drill each verb's stem-vowel change. The word-order pattern (modal in slot 2, infinitive at end) is identical for all six.

sections.title: The shared pattern of all six modals
sections.note: Three things are true for every modal verb. Internalise these once and they apply to all six.

blocks.text: 1. ich and er/sie/es have NO ending (and they're identical)
blocks.audioText: ich kann, er kann; ich muss, er muss; ich will, er will
blocks.rule: Unlike regular verbs (ich -e, er -t), modal verbs have ZERO ending in ich and er/sie/es — and the two forms are the same.

blocks.text: 2. The stem vowel often changes in singular forms (ich, du, er)
blocks.audioText: können → ich kann; wollen → ich will; müssen → ich muss
blocks.rule: können→kann, wollen→will, müssen→muss, dürfen→darf, mögen→mag. Only 'sollen' keeps its 'o' (ich soll). Plural forms (wir, ihr, sie) keep the infinitive stem.

blocks.text: 3. Modal sits at position 2 (V2 rule), the OTHER verb goes to the END in infinitive form
blocks.audioText: Ich kann Deutsch sprechen. Wir müssen morgen arbeiten.
blocks.rule: Two-verb structure: modal (conjugated, position 2) ... main verb (infinitive, sentence-final). This is the 'Klammer' (bracket) — verbs at both ends, everything else in between.

examples.translation: I can speak German. / Puedo hablar alemán.
We have to work tomorrow. / Tenemos que trabajar mañana.
He wants to read a book. / Él quiere leer un libro.

sections.title: The six modal verbs
sections.note: Drill each conjugation table. The examples show what kind of meaning each modal carries.

blocks.text: können — to be able to, can
blocks.audioText: können, ich kann, du kannst, er kann
blocks.translation: to be able to, can / poder, saber hacer algo
blocks.rule: Stem: kann- (singular), könn- (plural). Also expresses skills: 'Ich kann Deutsch' = I know German.

examples.translation: I can swim. / Sé nadar.
Can you help me? / ¿Puedes ayudarme?
They/You(formal) can come. / Pueden venir.

blocks.audioText: wollen, ich will, du willst, er will
blocks.rule: Stem: will- (singular!), woll- (plural). Note: 'ich will' = I WANT, NOT 'I will' (which is 'ich werde').

examples.translation: I want to learn German. / Quiero aprender alemán.
What do you want to eat? / ¿Qué quieres comer?
We want to go home. / Queremos ir a casa.

blocks.audioText: müssen, ich muss, du musst, er muss
blocks.translation: to have to, must / tener que, deber
blocks.rule: Stem: muss- (singular), müss- (plural with Umlaut). Expresses obligation or strong necessity.

examples.translation: I have to go now. / Tengo que irme ahora.
You have to drink water. / Tienes que beber agua.
We have to wait. / Tenemos que esperar.

blocks.audioText: sollen, ich soll, du sollst, er soll
blocks.translation: should, ought to / deber, se supone que
blocks.rule: The only modal that DOESN'T change its stem vowel. Conveys someone else's expectation or moral duty.

examples.translation: You shouldn't smoke. / No deberías fumar.
What should I do? / ¿Qué debo hacer?
He is supposed to be ill. (someone said so) / Dicen que está enfermo.

blocks.text: dürfen — to be allowed to, may
blocks.audioText: dürfen, ich darf, du darfst, er darf
blocks.translation: to be allowed to, may / poder (con permiso)
blocks.rule: Stem: darf- (singular), dürf- (plural). Use this when asking for or giving permission — NOT for ability.

examples.translation: May I sit here? / ¿Puedo sentarme aquí?
Children are not allowed to smoke. / Los niños no pueden fumar.
You may go. / Puedes irte.

blocks.text: mögen — to like (often used as 'möchten' for polite wishes)
blocks.audioText: mögen, ich mag, du magst, er mag; ich möchte, ich möchte einen Kaffee
blocks.translation: to like / gustar; möchten = would like / querer (cortés)
blocks.rule: Two uses: (1) 'mögen' = to like (usually with a noun, NO infinitive): 'Ich mag Schokolade'. (2) 'möchten' is a polite conditional form used as a verb in its own right: 'Ich möchte einen Kaffee' = I'd like a coffee. Memorise both — möchten is what you'll say constantly.

examples.translation: I like coffee. / Me gusta el café.
Do you like chocolate? / ¿Te gusta el chocolate?
I'd like a coffee, please. / Querría un café, por favor.
examples.ipa: möchten table: ich möchte, du möchtest, er möchte, wir möchten, ihr möchtet, sie möchten

examples.translation: What would you like to drink? (formal) / ¿Qué desea beber?

sections.title: Modal verbs in questions and negation
sections.note: Same V2 / verb-final pattern. For yes/no questions, the modal goes to position 1.

blocks.translation: Can you speak German? / ¿Puedes hablar alemán?
Do you have to work today? / ¿Tienes que trabajar hoy?
Shall we go to the cinema? / ¿Vamos al cine?
blocks.hint: 'Wollen wir...?' is the standard 'Shall we...?' suggestion.

blocks.translation: I can't come. / No puedo venir.
blocks.hint: 'nicht' usually goes right before the final infinitive.

blocks.translation: You are not allowed to park here. / No puedes aparcar aquí.
